abstract:
This study investigates the formation of secondary organic aerosol (SOA) from the oxidation of $\alpha$-pinene, comparing ozonolysis and hydroxyl radical (OH)-initiated pathways. Experiments were conducted in the University of North Carolina’s dual outdoor smog chamber facility under high nitric oxide (NO) conditions, with varying relative humidity (RH) and aerosol acidity. We found that ozonolysis produced significantly more SOA mass than OH-initiated oxidation under initially high-NO conditions, though RH had no discernible impact on the total SOA mass. Interestingly, when $\alpha$-pinene was oxidized by ozone in the presence of seed aerosol already coated with OH-derived organic matter, new particle nucleation was suppressed compared to experiments using pure ammonium sulfate seeds.

To understand these trends, we characterized the chemical composition of the SOA using UPLC/ESI-HR-Q-TOFMS, specifically targeting carboxylic acids and high-molecular-weight dimers. While eight carboxylic acids and four dimers were identified—accounting for 8–12% of the total SOA mass—their distribution varied strictly by oxidant. OH-initiated oxidation favored highly oxidized monomeric species like 3-methyl-1,2,3-butanetricarboxylic acid (MBTCA) and diaterpenylic acid acetate (DTAA). In contrast, dimers were only detected in SOA formed via ozonolysis. These dimers were twice as abundant at high RH (50–90%) compared to low RHed conditions (30–50%), a trend that correlates with increased new particle formation. Notably, aerosol acidity had a negligible effect on dimer concentrations.

Our results suggest that these dimers do not form through traditional particle-phase esterification, as previously thought. Instead, the data support a gas-phase mechanism involving stabilized Criegee Intermediates (sCI) produced during ozonolysis. The low volatility and high polarity of these gas-phase dimers likely drive homogeneous nucleation, increasing particle number concentrations. Given that three of these dimers (MW 358, 344, and 368) are frequently detected in ambient forest aerosols, we propose they serve as reliable molecular tracers for $\alpha$-pinene ozonolysis. Their presence in the atmosphere is a key indicator of ozone-driven aging processes and, by extension, the influence of anthropogenic pollution on biogenic aerosol formation.
